text: Welcome to another video. Today we're actually reviewing robotic cholecystectomy. This is an operation that I think many people use as a nice stepping stone as they build up their robotic experience and we'll highlight some of the nuances to the case. A sixty-year-old woman with a history of no significant surgical history presented with chronic abdominal pain. She had had multiple ultrasounds performed in the emergency department with gallstones, but no evidence of acute cholecystitis. She had repeatedly been discharged with referrals to surgeons that had not come to fruition, had a HIDA scan with an EF of less than ten percent. I saw her in my clinic and rescheduled her for a robotic cholecystectomy.

Whenever performing robotic colocystectomy, it's important to recognize that the setup is a little different than laparoscopic procedures. I personally like to use all eight millimeter ports. I tuck both arms, place the patient in a twenty percent reverse trim Dellenberg, place an OG tube. In the right lower quadrant, we'll use a force bipolar for retraction and dissection. In the right paramedian, we'll use a thirty degree camera. Umbilical port will be hook, colliery and monopolar scissors with an extraction through there. And then the left upper quadrant will be where we'll have the progress to retract the fundus.

The surgeon begins the standard dissection with minimal inflammation visible. The peritoneum is dissected on the lateral aspect of the artery, mobilizing it higher on the gallbladder. The lateral peritoneal dissection is developed further, aiding in the operation. The dissection continues underneath the gallbladder on the lateral side, revealing a twisted cystic duct that is identified and will be straightened through further dissection.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to precisely dissect and remove tissue between the cystic artery and duct, demonstrating efficient instrument handling in the robotic setup.

The surgeon works towards achieving the critical view of safety by dissecting off the cystic plate, extending the dissection higher than usual. Two distinct structures, the cystic duct and artery, are clearly visible entering the gallbladder. Clips are placed on these structures before transection.

The surgeon uses robotic monopolar scissors from the console to transect the gallbladder from the cystic plate, following extensive prior dissection. The gallbladder is then removed through the umbilical port using a retrieval bag.
